beats.prompt: Quick warm-up from L1 — match each term to what it does in the Bayes update.

pairs.right: rescale each hypothesis by how well it predicted the evidence
divide by the total over all hypotheses

feedback.correct: Rescale each hypothesis by how well it predicted the evidence, then renormalize. That move doesn't care whether there are two hypotheses or twenty.
hints: Warm-up from L1 — no pressure.
Posterior isn't the likelihood; it's prior × likelihood, divided by the total.
Rescale each by its likelihood, then divide by the sum over all of them.

beats.prompt: Machines M1 (50% of output, 1% defective), M2 (30%, 2%), M3 (20%, 3%). A part is defective. Which machine most likely made it?

options: Machine 1 — it makes the most parts (half of all output)
Machine 3 — it has the highest defect rate (3%)
You can't say from share or rate alone — weigh share × rate

Machine 1 — it makes the most parts (hal.note: M1 makes half of everything but errs only 1% of the time → just 5/17, the smallest of the three. Most output ≠ most blame.

Machine 3 — it has the highest defect ra.note: Closer — M3 ties for most likely. But not because its rate is highest: M2 (2% × 30%) ties M3 (3% × 20%) at 6/17. It's share × rate, not rate alone.

You can't say from share or rate alone —.note: Right instinct. Multiply each machine's share by its defect rate, then renormalize: M1 → 5/17, M2 = M3 → 6/17.

hints: No wrong guess — we'll weigh share × rate.
Most output isn't most blame; highest rate isn't either.
Per 10,000 parts: 50 : 60 : 60 defects → renormalize over 170.

beats.prompt: Read this just-in-time primer on the N-hypothesis denominator — then continue.

interaction.title: The denominator is a sum over all cases
interaction.body: With more than two hypotheses, the rule doesn't change — only the bookkeeping. The chance of the evidence is the sum of priorᵢ × Lᵢ over every machine (the law of total probability). Each posterior is that machine's share of the sum: posteriorᵢ = priorᵢ·Lᵢ / Σⱼ priorⱼ·Lⱼ.

feedback.correct: The denominator is a sum over every hypothesis — the law of total probability for N.
hints: Two hypotheses or ten — the rule is the same.
Each hypothesis contributes priorᵢ × Lᵢ to the total chance of the evidence.
posteriorᵢ = priorᵢ·Lᵢ / Σⱼ priorⱼ·Lⱼ.

beats.prompt: Watch three posterior bars renormalize from the share × rate products — Machine 1 starts with the biggest share, but ends up smallest.

hero.structuralReadout: Per 10,000 parts: Machine 1 makes 50 defects, Machines 2 and 3 each make 60 → 50 of 170 → 5/17 for Machine 1.

feedback.correct: Machine 1 makes half the parts but only 5/17 of the defects; Machines 2 and 3 tie at 6/17. Share alone doesn't decide — share × rate does, after renormalizing.
hints: Watch the prior bars (50 : 30 : 20) become posterior bars (5/17 : 6/17 : 6/17).
The biggest prior shrinks because its defect rate is the lowest.
Each bar = that machine's share of the 170 defects per 10,000 parts.

beats.prompt: Per 10,000 parts: M1 runs 5,000 @ 1%, M2 runs 3,000 @ 2%, M3 runs 2,000 @ 3%. What fraction of defects came from Machine 1?
beats.interviewNote: Which-source / n-hypothesis defect is a standard Bayes interview question (interview pack `multi-source-defect`): weight each source's share by its defect rate, then renormalize by the law of total probability. The trap is comparing the raw products (50 : 60 : 60) without dividing by 170.

feedback.correct: Per 10,000 parts: 50 from M1, 60 from M2, 60 from M3 — total 170. P(M1 | defective) = 50/170 = 5/17.
hints: That's 50 : 60 : 60. The products aren't probabilities until you divide by the total 170 (the law of total probability).

beats.prompt: Same 170 total defects. Compute P(Machine 3 | defective) and P(Machine 2 | defective). Do they tie?

feedback.correct: P(M3) = 60/170 = 6/17 — and P(M2) = 60/170 = 6/17 too. M2 and M3 tie even though M2 runs 30% and M3 only 20%: a higher rate exactly offsets a smaller share.
hints: Both land on 60 of 170 — the products are equal, so the posteriors are equal.

beats.prompt: Match each machine's headline factor to why it loses — and what actually decides.

pairs.right: still only 5/17 — its defect rate is the lowest
only 6/17 — its share is the smallest
share × rate, then renormalize over the total

feedback.correct: Neither the biggest share nor the highest rate wins alone — the product does. That's L2's base-rate lesson in a new costume.
hints: Recall L2: a big prior can outweigh a strong likelihood — and vice versa.
M1 has the most share but the lowest rate; M3 has the highest rate but the least share.
Share × rate, then renormalize — that's the only thing that decides.

beats.prompt: Three methods all converge on 5/17 — reveal each lens.

lenses.label: Frequencies
lenses.body: Per 10,000 parts: 50 : 60 : 60 defects; Machine 1 is 50 of 170

feedback.correct: Formula, the 170-defect count, and the odds ratio all land on 5/17 — not an artifact of one method.
hints: Reveal each lens.
They are three roads to the same number.
All three say 5/17.

beats.prompt: Mastery challenge: three new suppliers — S1 makes 25% of parts at a 5% defect rate, S2 makes 20% at 3%, S3 makes 55% at 1%. A part arrives defective. What is P(Supplier 1 | defective)?

interaction.scenario: Three suppliers: \(S_1\) makes 25% of parts at a 5% defect rate, \(S_2\) makes 20% at 3%, \(S_3\) makes 55% at 1%. A part arrives defective.

feedback.correct: Apply the N-hypothesis formula: \[ P(S_1\mid\text{defective}) = \frac{P(S_1)\,P(\text{defective}\mid S_1)}{\sum_{j=1}^{3} P(S_j)\,P(\text{defective}\mid S_j)} \] Per 10,000 parts, compute share \(\times\) defect rate for each supplier: \(S_1\colon 2500\times 5\%=125\), \(S_2\colon 2000\times 3\%=60\), \(S_3\colon 5500\times 1\%=55\); total \(=240\). Substituting: \[ P(S_1\mid\text{defective}) = \frac{125}{240} = \frac{25}{48} \] \(S_1\) produces the most defects despite making only 25% of all parts — a high defect rate beats a bigger share when the two are multiplied.
hints: Apply the N-hypothesis rule: weight each supplier's share by its defect rate, then renormalize over the total.

beats.prompt: Reveal the recap to consolidate the N-way rule.

feedback.correct: The two-hypothesis rule is the rule for N: posteriorᵢ = priorᵢ·Lᵢ / Σⱼ priorⱼ·Lⱼ. Rescale every hypothesis by how well it predicted the evidence, then renormalize by the total.
hints: A defective part most likely came from neither the highest-output nor the highest-rate machine — it's the product that ranks them.
M2 and M3 tie at 6/17 because share × rate is equal even though shares differ.
Next up: Monty Hall — three doors, and the host's action is the evidence.
